texto: Appetite knows what it craves, without cerebral embellishment. It tends not to waste any time laying hold of its tools. That was the thing I had recognised here: appetite. I recognised it precisely because, in a context like this, it was so unfamiliar. It had forced me to rule out everything else. And there was a second reason for my recognition, which because unprecedented was not recognition at all, but astounding discovery: Martha's face told me. I saw appetite there...
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Susan Choi: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de passagem do tempo e prioridades. O trecho “Appetite knows what it craves, without cerebral embellishment. It tends not to waste any time lay…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que tempo, attraction, desire pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de termino e desire.

And there was a second reason for my recognition, which because unprecedented was not recognition at all, but astounding discovery: Martha's face told me. I saw appetite there...

texto: I think 'Gatsby' is hobbled, in part, by its status as a Great American Novel. People kind of roll their eyes before they've even opened it, treat it with a 'been there, done that' attitude. I know I did. It took me years to re-open the novel and see how much I'd missed.
explicacao: Citação de Susan Choi sobre temas ligados a reflexao. “I think 'Gatsby' is hobbled, in part, by its status as a Great American Novel. People kind of rol…” articula reflexao, great, eyes em registro de reflexao, com relevância para contextos como eyes e american.

frase_original: I think 'Gatsby' is hobbled, in part, by its status as a Great American Novel. People kind of roll their eyes before they've even opened it, treat it with a 'been there, done that' attitude. I know I did. It took me years to re-open the novel and see how much I'd missed.

texto: I didn't grasp that desire and duty could rival each other, least of all that they most often did.
